findings.reasoning: California involuntary termination with final pay date (2026-03-22) occurring 7 days after last work date (2026-03-15). CA law requires final wages be paid immediately on last day of work for involuntary terminations. Additionally, employee has recent complaint on file and termination is for 'performance' without documented PIP, creating potential retaliation claim. This mirrors the customer's 2024-Q3 CA final pay issue.
recommended_actions: URGENT: Verify if final pay was actually issued on 2026-03-15; if delayed, calculate waiting time penalties
Review complaint details and timeline vs. performance documentation
Assess whether complaint relates to protected class or activity
Consider proactive payment of any penalties owed
Document business rationale for performance termination independent of complaint

findings.reasoning: Voluntary resignation in Texas with final pay date (2026-03-29) matching last work date, which complies with TX requirements (within 6 days for involuntary, and this is voluntary). Employee has no active legal issues, complaints, or FMLA. Relatively short tenure (1.1 years) is not unusual for voluntary departure.
recommended_actions: Confirm final pay was issued as scheduled

findings.reasoning: California involuntary performance-based termination of employee with ACTIVE FMLA leave. Terminating an employee on active FMLA is a serious compliance violation and retaliation risk under federal law, regardless of documentation of PIP. While final pay date (2026-03-16) correctly matches last work date per CA law, and a documented PIP exists, the FMLA interference issue is critical. Additionally, this is the second Engineering department involuntary termination within 24 hours, requiring WARN Act analysis.
recommended_actions: URGENT: Conduct legal review of FMLA compliance; this may require immediate remediation
Verify FMLA leave status at time of termination decision
Obtain full PIP documentation and timeline to establish legitimate, independent reason
Determine if other terminations/layoffs occurred in same 30-day period for WARN Act assessment
Preserve all communications related to performance management and FMLA interaction
Consider whether FMLA restoration of position or severance negotiation may mitigate exposure

findings.reasoning: Standard health benefits modification in response to qualifying life event (birth). Effective date (2026-04-01) is 18 days after change request (2026-03-14), well within 30-day window for life event enrollments. Coverage level change from individual to family is expected for birth event. No COBRA triggers, employee remains active.
recommended_actions: Confirm benefits system processed change correctly
Verify birth documentation on file if required by plan

customer_insights: Acme Corp shows concerning pattern in this batch: two involuntary terminations in Engineering (high-turnover department) within 24 hours, both in California (location with prior compliance issues), both with performance reasons but varying documentation levels. Most critically, evt-003 involves FMLA-protected employee, which is a serious legal risk. Evt-001 has final pay timing violation that mirrors the 2024-Q3 CA issue, suggesting possible recurring process gap or insufficient CA compliance training. Recommend: (1) Immediate legal review of both CA terminations, especially FMLA case; (2) Full 30-day termination audit for WARN Act applicability given ~2,400 employee size; (3) CA payroll process audit to prevent repeat final pay violations; (4) Engineering department exit interview analysis to understand turnover drivers.
